text: The video shows a paraesophageal hernia repair with a toupee fundoplication. The surgeon begins by dissecting into the hernia sac space, pulling the hernia sac down to find the space between the hernia sac and the mediastinum. A space is developed between the stomach and esophagus below, and the mediastinum containing the heart above. On the right side, the surgeon frees the attachments to the esophagus, retracting it on the left and working around it to free the mediastinal attachments. The aorta is visible below the dissection, and the surgeon ligates the short gastric blood vessels.

The surgeon frees the stomach from the diaphragm and places a Penrose drain around the gastroesophageal junction to aid in retracting the esophagus. The surgical field shows the beating heart in the mediastinum above the esophagus. The surgeon works on the right posterior aspect of the esophagus, near the aorta, preserving the vagus nerve while addressing a wide hiatal defect. Suturing is performed to snugly oppose the hiatus around the esophagus using permanent braided sutures, with stitches placed posteriorly and occasionally anteriorly. The esophagus rests with the hiatus nicely opposed, eliminating any hiatal defects.

The surgeon performs a posterior 270-degree Toupee fundoplication by bringing the fundus behind the esophagus from left to right. Both sides are grasped, and the shoeshine maneuver is used to ensure no twisting occurs. Suturing of the fundoplication is completed using Ethabon sutures, first on the left side and then on the right. Additional sutures anchor the gastroesophageal junction and fundoplication to the diaphragm and the posterior aspect of the crura to prevent reherniation. The final appearance of the Toupee fundoplication is shown after the paraesophageal hernia repair.
